two_body_problem.title: Optimal Initial Positioning
two_body_problem.principle: Placing the red ball at a strategic horizontal offset ensures the green ball is pushed towards the blue ball, facilitating contact.
two_body_problem.when_to_apply: When the green and blue balls are horizontally separated and falling under gravity.

two_body_problem.title: Consider Mass and Radius
two_body_problem.principle: The red ball's mass and radius influence its ability to push the green ball effectively; larger mass and radius increase the force and duration of contact.
two_body_problem.when_to_apply: When the green and blue balls have different sizes and masses, ensuring the red ball is appropriately sized for the task.

two_body_problem.title: Utilize Gravity for Timing
two_body_problem.principle: Using the natural downward acceleration due to gravity allows precise timing of the red ball's placement to ensure sustained contact between the green and blue balls.
two_body_problem.when_to_apply: When the exact timing of the red ball's placement is critical for maintaining contact for 3 seconds.

two_body_problem.title: Relative Position Analysis
two_body_problem.principle: Analyzing the relative positions and velocities of the green and blue balls helps determine the optimal placement and velocity for the red ball to achieve the desired outcome.
two_body_problem.when_to_apply: When the initial positions and velocities of the green and blue balls are complex and varied.

two_body_problem.title: Avoid Overcomplicating the Solution
two_body_problem.principle: Simple and direct solutions often outperform overly complex strategies, as they are more likely to be robust and effective.
two_body_problem.when_to_apply: When multiple attempts with complex strategies fail, simplifying the approach can lead to success.

two_body_problem.title: Relative Position Analysis
two_body_problem.principle: Using the `compute_relative_positions` tool to understand the relative distances and angles between the green and blue balls helps in determining the correct placement for the red ball.
two_body_problem.when_to_apply: When the initial positions and velocities of the green and blue balls are known and need to be analyzed for effective interaction.

two_body_problem.title: Initial Velocity Consideration
two_body_problem.principle: Considering the initial velocities of the balls when placing the red ball ensures that the push is effective and timely.
two_body_problem.when_to_apply: When the initial velocities of the green and blue balls are non-zero, and the agent needs to account for their movement.

two_body_problem.description: Placing the red ball too far away horizontally from the green ball.
two_body_problem.why_it_happens: The agent incorrectly assumes that a large horizontal distance is necessary to generate a significant push, ignoring the importance of timing and vertical alignment.
two_body_problem.how_to_avoid: Place the red ball closer to the green ball, ensuring it has enough time to interact before the balls fall out of range.

two_body_problem.description: Not considering the vertical alignment of the balls when placing the red ball.
two_body_problem.why_it_happens: The agent focuses solely on horizontal positioning, neglecting the fact that the red ball needs to be at a similar vertical level to effectively push the green ball.
two_body_problem.how_to_avoid: Ensure the red ball is placed at a similar vertical level as the green ball to maximize the push effect.

two_body_problem.description: Incorrectly assuming the red ball will always push the green ball towards the blue ball regardless of its initial velocity.
two_body_problem.why_it_happens: The agent does not account for the initial velocities and positions of the balls, leading to incorrect placements that fail to achieve the desired outcome.
two_body_problem.how_to_avoid: Simulate the action first to check if the red ball's placement and initial conditions result in the green ball moving towards the blue ball.
